nodes.label: Cycle archive — the forge self-closure arc
Cycle archive — trafficGame world-map review arc (2026-05-17 → 2026-05-18)
Cycle archive — trafficGame collision/elevation + grading-frontier arc
ADR 001 — Claude Agent SDK as the agent runtime
ADR 002 — Ralph loop pattern over Claude Agent SDK
ADR 003 — All "agents" are Claude Code skills
ADR 004 — Brain is a Karpathy three-layer wiki rendered in Obsidian
ADR 005 — Phase isolation with per-phase benchmarks
ADR 006 — gh CLI + git worktrees + GitHub Actions
ADR 008 — JSONL event log per cycle
ADR 009 — Minimal forge.config.json; settings live in skills/ADRs
ADR 011 — Unattended scheduler with file-based initiative queue and worktree pool
ADR 012 — Crash recovery via worktree heartbeat + atomic claim
trafficGame — source docs ingest snapshot (2026-05-10)
nodes.description: Verbatim concatenation of projects/trafficGame/docs/MVP_ARCHITECTURE.md and projects/trafficGame/docs/LEARNINGS.md as captured before the first user-augmented architect run. Themes under brain/projects/trafficGame/themes/ summarise this raw layer.

nodes.label: Work item completion rates by domain complexity
v1 wiki extracts — cost optimisation lessons
v1 wiki extracts — design + merge lessons
v1 wiki extracts — failure modes + recovery patterns
OpenClaw and Hermes Agent — brief profiles
The Ralph Wiggum Loop: Core Pattern (Geoffrey Huntley)
The Ralph Wiggum Technique: Origins, Spread, and Impact
Agent stuck without detection — silent build/test loops
nodes.description: 12 developer timeouts/day at $8-12 waste in v1, agents cycling through the same build failure with no file changes. >5 turns no-progress is the heuristic; capture diagnostic on timeout.

nodes.label: Alternative loop runtimes — Aider, OpenHands, OpenClaw, Hermes
nodes.description: Reference profiles for loop runtimes forge could swap to. Each rejected at scaffold for shape-mismatch reasons; loops/_adapters/ holds future adapter slots.

nodes.label: Avoid hand-rolling tools that battle-tested community equivalents exist for
nodes.description: User principle 1 — plug into Claude, gh CLI, git worktree, Ralph loop, etc. The user's idea is in hanging powerful ideas together, not building from scratch.

nodes.label: Brain-first research
nodes.description: Every skill mandates brain-query as its first action. Broader research only when the brain is insufficient — and the gap is logged.

nodes.label: Brain-gap feedback loop
nodes.description: brain-query logs unanswered questions to brain-gaps.jsonl; brain-ingest fills them at end of cycle. The brain learns from its own failures.

nodes.label: Brain-read policy — planner reads, executor/reviewer don't, all reads index-guarded
nodes.description: Operator-confirmed. The architect/PM MUST read the brain (antipatterns + historical work-sizing inform initiative slicing). The dev-loop and reviewer MUST NOT — intent is wholly captured in the work items the planner authored. Every brain read must use the INDEX/category-index/profile metadata, not expensive full scans.

nodes.label: An e2e test is a seed at the front of the chain — not a benchmark with its own rubric
nodes.description: Keep the isolated per-phase benches. "Chained" means each phase bench's generated output is the next phase bench's input, scored by the existing per-phase pure rubrics. An end-to-end test is a SEED fed into the architect bench, never a standalone fixture+rubric. Eliminate benchmarks/e2e's bespoke rubric; keep only its plumbing in _lib. Plus 3 isolated-bench drift corrections.

nodes.label: Claude Agent SDK as the agent runtime
nodes.description: First-party @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk powers every TS-side agent invocation in forge v2.

nodes.label: Claude Agent SDK hooks system
nodes.description: PreToolUse / PostToolUse / SessionStart / UserPromptSubmit lifecycle hooks. Inspect/modify tool calls, augment prompts, inject context, block dangerous operations.

nodes.label: Claude Agent SDK subagents
nodes.description: Specialised agents declared inline in options.agents, invoked by the main agent via the Agent tool. Each has its own tools, model, maxTurns, permissionMode. Can run background.

nodes.label: Conditional core-values injection per role
nodes.description: Each agent role receives only the subset of core values relevant to its job. Smaller stable prefix → better cache hits + less token waste on irrelevant context.

nodes.label: Cost-aware model routing — Opus for design, Sonnet for coding, Haiku for triage
nodes.description: Different tasks have different reasoning depths. Per-skill model overrides in forge.config.json route triage to Haiku, coding to Sonnet, deep design to Opus.

nodes.label: Crash recovery via worktree heartbeat + atomic claim
nodes.description: Two file-system passes at scheduler startup recover orphaned in-flight initiatives. No DB, no journaling, no leader election.

nodes.label: Cycle event-log replay
nodes.description: events.jsonl + referenced artifacts let any cycle be replayed for reflection, debugging, or benchmark fixture creation.

nodes.label: Declarative specs over imperative instructions
nodes.description: Ralph principle — describe the desired state, let the agent iterate toward it, rather than dictating step-by-step procedures. Bad specs produce mediocre results.

nodes.label: Dependency-ordered work — parallelism is correctness, not optimisation
nodes.description: When work items don't declare dependencies, parallel execution silently corrupts merges. depends_on edges + a graph-critic make parallelism safe.

nodes.label: Design is the bottleneck
nodes.description: Planner quality multiplies everything downstream — agents churn through implementation fast; bad work-item design produces churn, failed completions, and wasted tokens that better design would prevent.

nodes.label: Episodic-not-cumulative learnings antipattern
nodes.description: 5 identical learnings files generated in a single day for the same observation, because cross-cycle context was truncated. The wiki is the architectural fix; deduplication-at-write is a tactical patch.

nodes.label: Eval-driven development — every change shows a benchmark delta
nodes.description: If a change can't move a benchmark, you don't know if it helped. Per-phase benchmarks + per-PR delta + reflection-discovered failures become new cases.

nodes.label: Exploration initiatives differ structurally from implementation initiatives — the architect/PM/dev-loop pipeline is shaped for the latter and needs new artifacts for the former
nodes.description: The trafficGame collision/elevation arc (PR

nodes.label: File-based state machine for queue management
nodes.description: _queue/{pending,in-flight,ready-for-review,done,failed}/ directories with atomic mv as the transition primitive. The filesystem IS the protocol.

nodes.label: Forge as-built — 5 wired phases + hand-run architect, PM/reflector-only brain-first, ~4,400 LOC, a real resilience layer
nodes.description: Honest snapshot of forge's actual structure (not the stated ideal). Architect is out-of-cycle Path-B; brain-first is enforced only for PM+reflector; orchestrator is ~4,400 LOC not 300; the classifier/auto-retry resilience layer is the most mature subsystem; queue state is not ground truth.

nodes.label: Forge never self-modifies while running
nodes.description: Reflection outputs recommendations; a human implements forge changes in a separate session after the forge process is stopped. CI validates before forge restarts.

nodes.label: The forge↔project contract — six clauses a project must meet for unattended progress
nodes.description: Empirically derived from what trafficGame needed before forge could run it unattended. C1 fast gate, C2 scratch hygiene, C3 decomposed source, C4 machine-readable arch context, C5 honoured locked-core mandates, C6 satisfiable merge model (the open clause).

nodes.label: gh CLI + git worktrees + GitHub Actions
nodes.description: Use battle-tested git/GitHub tooling instead of hand-rolled equivalents. Worktrees give native filesystem isolation per parallel work unit.

nodes.label: gstack conventions — process-not-tools, skill-driven, markdown-artifact-flow
nodes.description: 23 sprint-phase skills + SKILL.md/SKILL.md.tmpl + /autoplan + /learn. Inspired forge v2's skills-as-agents and markdown-artifact-flow.

nodes.label: Post-merge health-check protocol
nodes.description: After every gh pr merge, run language-agnostic test discovery + execute tests in a worktree-isolated environment. The merge step is not the success condition; the test suite on updated main is.

nodes.label: Holistic project metrics are an onboarding contract clause — without them, agentic ideation is blind
nodes.description: The forge↔project contract (C1 fast gate, C2 scratch hygiene, C3 decomposed source, C4 machine-readable arch, C5 honoured locked-core, C6 satisfiable merge model) is necessary but not sufficient for measurement-driven loops. A new clause — C7 holistic metrics, with a measurement command and locked baselines — is the missing piece. Tests verify "did this break"; metrics verify "did this help".

nodes.label: Hand-directed project surgery routed through the pipeline blurs autonomy metrics
nodes.description: simplification-tests/source/arch and hand-authored reflection themes were human-directed trafficGame surgery run as forge initiatives. "Forge autonomously progresses the project" and "human directs specific work via the pipeline" have different success criteria; the queue/metrics do not distinguish them.

nodes.label: Human interaction points run in the operator's own Claude session via slash commands
nodes.description: The three deliberate human moments (roadmap/architect, review feedback & merge, reflection feedback) must be undertaken in the operator's own Claude session (CLI / VSCode extension), not a forge-spawned agent. Implement as slash commands. Forge must never simulate these in production.

nodes.label: JSONL event log per cycle
nodes.description: One append-only events.jsonl per cycle records every skill invocation, iteration, cost, duration. Source of truth for reflection, metrics, replay.

nodes.label: Karpathy three-layer LLM wiki
nodes.description: Brain layout — immutable raw → 15-40-line theme pages → category indexes. Many small pages > few large summaries.

nodes.label: Layered merge order with health checks
nodes.description: Stacked PRs merge in strict Layer 0 → Layer 1 → Layer 2 order, with a post-merge health check between each layer. The merge train stops on failure; human review unblocks.

nodes.label: LLM Council pattern — chained-reviewer perspectives
nodes.description: Multi-perspective critic chain (CEO/eng/design/DX) auto-resolves mechanical questions and surfaces only taste decisions to the user. Inspired by gstack /autoplan.

nodes.label: Markdown artifacts flow phase-to-phase
nodes.description: All inter-phase data is markdown with YAML frontmatter. Greppable, debuggable, version-controllable. Inspired by gstack.

nodes.label: Merge boundary is forge's real unattended ceiling — stacked initiatives strand in done/
nodes.description: 12 trafficGame cycles; only PR

nodes.label: Minimal forge.config.json
nodes.description: Per-machine, gitignored, ~10 lines. Models, projectsDir, scheduler concurrency, notify provider. Everything else lives in ADRs, SKILL.md, or initiative manifests.

nodes.label: Autonomous closure works when the stop condition is an objective script, not the agent's judgement
nodes.description: A confirmed plan was driven to a green gate by an in-session loop + fresh-context subagents, each unit gated tsc+tests before commit. The loop could not self-declare done — closure-check.ts (tiered fast|full, parsed from a coverage matrix) was the sole arbiter. Closure 2/22→25/25 fast, 30/31 full; cycle.ts 1753→330; 0 regressions.

nodes.label: Parametric design search — the ideation-fanout pattern any project with a measurable outcome can use
nodes.description: trafficGame's runSweep.mjs library is ~250 lines of reusable infrastructure that turned every map-design hypothesis from prose into a ~30-line script + 10s wall-clock sweep. Pattern generalises to any project where a parameter space and a holistic metric exist; forge should expose a project-agnostic harness skeleton.

nodes.label: Phase isolation with per-phase benchmarks
nodes.description: Every phase has benchmarks/<phase>/ with sample inputs → measurable outputs so improvement is provable per-phase without a full cycle.

nodes.label: Pluggable notifications on review-ready
nodes.description: orchestrator/notify.ts with desktop (default) and webhook providers. Closes the unattended loop — human knows when to look.

nodes.label: The PR must be the sole review window (visibility-aware demo-in-PR)
nodes.description: When the operator is engaged, iterating via PR comments is a low-overhead high-fidelity review loop — but ONLY if the PR is self-contained. The demo must live IN the PR; for PRIVATE repos that means a committed DEMO.md with relative image links, not inline raw URLs (GitHub's image proxy can't fetch private raw).

nodes.label: Prompt caching — stable prefix first
nodes.description: Structuring system prompts with stable content first achieved 92% cache hits in v1 Cycle 3 (903M reads vs 363K fresh). The single largest cost lever in the token budget.

nodes.label: Quality gates verified by the orchestrator, not the agent
nodes.description: Acceptance-criterion verification runs in orchestrator (npm test, gh pr checks), not in the agent's response. Defends against hallucinated test passes.

nodes.label: Ralph loop pattern
nodes.description: ~30-line outer loop where iteration lives in the loop itself, not the orchestrator. Used as forge v2's developer loop.

nodes.label: Ralph stop-hook pattern vs bash-loop pattern
nodes.description: Two implementations of Ralph — outer bash loop (ghuntley canonical) vs Stop-hook within a single Claude Code session (Anthropic plugin). Different operational properties.

nodes.label: Rate-limit no-backoff antipattern
nodes.description: Retrying spawns immediately after hitting Claude's rate limit produced 215+ zero-cost spawns and 49% of all v1 Cycle 3 job failures. Parse resetsAt, set rateLimitPausedUntil, gate canSpawnAgent.

nodes.label: Agent-facing constraints masked structural bugs — diagnose cwd/state before adding gates
nodes.description: F-35..F-43 arc — the PM path validator, per-WI brain-first gate, brain index, noop stop condition all got ripped out. The real bugs were cwd=forgeRoot (F-37) and shared Ralph scratch (F-40). Constraints added false-positive surface and hid root causes.

nodes.label: Review-fix loop spinning — SHA-guard missing
nodes.description: One v1 Cycle 1 merge produced 70+ review-fix cycles overnight. 40-60% of $451 Cycle 1 spend wasted. SHA-change guard + worktree isolation + chain consolidation are the fix.

nodes.label: Review-phase target design — initiative branch synced, holistic intent gate, PR-as-feedback-surface, no auto-merge
nodes.description: Operator's authoritative redesign. Dev-loop keeps the initiative branch in local↔remote sync and creates NO PR. Review = automated loop that assesses the branch against initiative INTENT holistically, may spawn dev-loops to refine, then emits a demo-embedded PR. The GitHub PR is the human feedback/merge surface; closure aligns local to remote.

nodes.label: Roadmaps converge on simplification before features
nodes.description: All 4 v1 project roadmaps independently chose simplify-and-consolidate before adding new capabilities. The planner has a reliable instinct for structural health — useful default for the architect.

nodes.label: Simplicity is key — every "no" defends it
nodes.description: User principle 2. Forge is a small core that hangs powerful tools together. The non-goals lists across ADRs are load-bearing.

nodes.label: Six phases of forge backed by a brain
nodes.description: Brain → Architect → Project Manager → Developer Loop → Review Loop → Reflection. Three human-in-the-loop touchpoints; everything else unattended.

nodes.label: Skills as the agent surface
nodes.description: Every "agent" in forge v2 is a Claude Code skill (SKILL.md per role) — no spawned subprocesses, no custom registry.

nodes.label: Spec-driven development — PRD as the contract
nodes.description: Vague specs propagate downstream and break the developer loop. Concrete specs let agents self-verify. PRD is the contract between architect → PM → developer.

nodes.label: Spec-driven work items
nodes.description: Atomic (≤3 files), Given-When-Then acceptance criteria, declared dependencies, designed for iteration not one-shotting. PM phase output.

nodes.label: Squash-merging stacked PRs is forbidden
nodes.description: v1 Cycle 2 trafficGame produced 90 test failures + 12 TS errors after 8 stacked PRs were squash-merged independently. Use layered merge order with health checks instead.

nodes.label: TDD with agents — tests first, verified in a worktree
nodes.description: Agents produce plausible-looking code that doesn't quite work. Tests make correctness mechanically verifiable. Orchestrator runs the gate, not the agent.

nodes.label: Theme page format
nodes.description: 15-40 line markdown file with mandatory frontmatter, ≥1 source link, ≤60 lines (warn) / 100 (error). Slug = filename. Indexed in exactly one category index.

nodes.label: Unattended scheduler with file-based queue + worktree pool
nodes.description: ~150-line forge serve loop. _queue/ directories + atomic mv for claim + bounded git worktrees. The filesystem IS the protocol.

nodes.label: v1 vs v2 — what was rejected and why
nodes.description: V2 keeps v1's mental models (TDD, dep-ordered work, brain-as-wiki) and replaces v1's hand-rolled infrastructure with battle-tested community tools.

nodes.label: Wedged loop detector
nodes.description: One of Ralph's stop conditions — abort when the loop makes no progress for N iterations. Caps token burn on no-op iterations.

nodes.label: Wiki over truncated learning-context
nodes.description: Forge v1 truncated cross-cycle learnings to 2000 chars per cycle, producing episodic-not-cumulative memory. The Karpathy three-layer wiki is the architectural fix — and the load-bearing argument for v2's brain.

nodes.label: Work-item completion by domain complexity
nodes.description: Domain complexity — not item count or codebase size — is the primary predictor of agent failure rate. Clean Python achieves 3.6 min avg; complex TS canvas takes 5.2 min with 26.9 min outliers.

nodes.label: GitWeave — control-repo centricity is non-negotiable
nodes.description: All behaviour drives from one repo. No bespoke services, no parallel control planes. Initiatives that propose a separate "GitWeave service" are escalations, not auto-resolves.

nodes.label: GitWeave — local dry-run (gw:plan) required for every CLI tool
nodes.description: Every change-introducing CLI must support a local dry-run. "Trust the agent, run the apply" is forbidden — Terraform-style plan-then-apply is the discipline.

nodes.label: GitWeave — scattered branches debt; consolidate before features
nodes.description: 6 unmerged branches at end of v1 Cycle 3. Roadmap says "simplify and consolidate" first. Architect should reject feature initiatives that add to the pile.

nodes.label: env-optimiser — local-first, zero network calls (constitutional)
nodes.description: Constitution principle 1. All data lives in ~/.wsl-deo/. Any network call requires explicit constitutional justification — privacy is the load-bearing trust property.

nodes.label: env-optimiser — secret redaction is mandatory before any storage
nodes.description: 30+ patterns redacted before SQLite write. A capture path that bypasses redaction is a critical bug, not a feature toggle.

nodes.label: env-optimiser — features land via specs/<feature>/ pattern (specify/)
nodes.description: Each feature has spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md, quickstart.md under specs/<NNN>-<slug>/. The PM phase mirrors this layout when decomposing initiatives.

nodes.label: healarr — email approvals; no web UI
nodes.description: Correct-tier actions are gated by email approval — reply "approve" / "yes" or "reject" / "no" as the first line. Zero web infrastructure. Works from any phone.

nodes.label: healarr — triage stage is deterministic; LLM only in agent stage
nodes.description: Two-stage pipeline. Triage = rule engine over observations; cheap, fast, no LLM. Agent stage runs only on the small fraction triage marks suspicious. Don't blur the boundary.

nodes.label: simplarr — docker-compose-{nas,pi}.yml are profiles, not duplicates
nodes.description: Host-specific overrides on a shared base. A new profile needs justification, not just a copy. Changes to one require considering both.

nodes.label: simplarr — Bash + PowerShell parity is mandatory
nodes.description: Every configurator feature exists in both configure.sh and configure.ps1. Single-language work items are flagged as missing a sibling by the dependency-graph critic.

nodes.label: simplarr — no monitoring or remediation logic (that's healarr)
nodes.description: simplarr is set-up-and-run only. Adding monitoring, remediation, or self-healing logic is a new project (healarr), not a simplarr feature.

nodes.label: trafficGame — campaign is a connected CampaignGraph (5-map directed convergent-AND world)
nodes.description: The level array is long gone. The campaign is a CampaignGraph of 5 maps with DIRECTED edges carrying real, count-parity-validated connection points. Unlock is convergent-AND (a map unlocks only when EVERY map feeding it is completed; sources always unlocked). The hub renders a spatial map-of-maps with two-way-road exit/entry ports. This is what any campaign/world/unlock initiative starts from now.

nodes.label: trafficGame — developer-ralph skips brain-first on second WI in multi-WI runs
nodes.description: In multi-WI cycles, developer-ralph reliably consulted the brain for WI-1 but skipped it entirely for WI-2, triggering an automatic fail; the brain-first mandate must be enforced per-WI, not per-cycle.

nodes.label: trafficGame — minimal pure-utility initiatives complete in 1 developer iteration
nodes.description: Tightly-scoped pure-function additions to core utilities (single file, concrete numeric acceptance criteria, strict TypeScript) reliably complete in 1 developer-ralph iteration with no wedge events.

nodes.label: trafficGame — MVP architecture snapshot (May 2026, post-extraction)
nodes.description: Current src/ tree, key extracted services (NetworkAnalysisService, NetworkOptimizer split, VisibilityManager, GameToolManager), and what survives unchanged from the MVP doc. Use this to ground any architect proposal in the real present-day shape.

nodes.label: trafficGame — recent extractions reduced Game.ts and what tech debt remains
nodes.description: Apr–May 2026 work split NetworkOptimizer, extracted NetworkAnalysisService/VisibilityManager/GameToolManager out of Game.ts, archived MCP server, removed 264-line dead TrafficPointEntity. Game.ts is still ~1,732 lines and TrafficMap.ts is still 517 — these are the priority extractions left.

nodes.label: trafficGame — review-loop overhead dominates cost for trivial initiatives
nodes.description: For simple utility additions, the reviewer phase (2 iterations, demo scaffolding, PR description) consumed 53% of total cycle cost, more than PM (28%) and developer (19%) combined; fixed review scaffolding cost is disproportionate to implementation complexity.

nodes.label: trafficGame — test stack is Vitest + Playwright with a strict TDD + visual-test discipline
nodes.description: TypeScript strict, Vitest for unit/integration, Playwright for visual; locked CLAUDE.md mandates write-tests-first, ~150 lines per file, no any, no time-based waits. Quality gates for any forge initiative against trafficGame must include both npm test and npm run test:visual where canvas/physics is touched.

nodes.label: trafficGame — TrafficLocation has paired entry/exit ConnectionPoints with edge orientation
nodes.description: TrafficPoint.ts defines TrafficLocation as a single map-edge anchor with separate entryPoint and exitPoint ConnectionPoints offset by edge direction (top/bottom/left/right). This is the natural seam for any "link maps at entry/exit" non-linear campaign work.

nodes.label: trafficGame — traffic physics, IDM model, and BPR flow prediction
nodes.description: How cars accelerate, brake, and follow each other in trafficGame; the IDM-derived constants; the BPR-based flow prediction; and what's known to work and not work today. Includes the leverage points for any "smarter braking + intersection back-pressure" initiative.

nodes.label: trafficGame — UI overlays paint translucent black via fillRect without clearRect first
nodes.description: GameMenu.draw() (and likely other CanvasScreen-derived overlays) paint rgba(0,0,0,0.7) full-canvas without clearing first. Each redraw stacks another translucent layer on whatever is underneath — this is the most likely cause of the "menu hover incrementally darkens the screen" bug.

nodes.label: trafficGame — demo Playwright config reuseExistingServer:true captures stale main-repo build
nodes.description: The Playwright demo config used reuseExistingServer:true and latched onto a pre-existing vite dev server from the main repo rather than the worktree build. Every screenshot in the demo bundle showed the pre-change hub (3-node linear chain), making the demo actively misleading. Per-worktree server isolation is required.

nodes.label: trafficGame — one-file-per-WI constraint correlated with single-iteration dev-loop success
nodes.description: Manifest hard constraints (FEAT-1 → campaignGraphData.ts, FEAT-2 → CampaignGraph.ts, FEAT-3 → CampaignHub.ts; no shared files across features) produced 3 dev-loop WIs that each completed in a single iteration with no wedge events, no send-backs, and 836 tests green. File-isolation avoids the file-collision failure mode seen in prior trafficGame cycles.

nodes.label: trafficGame — reviewer per-iteration budget undersized for medium-complexity initiatives
nodes.description: REVIEWER_LIVE_MAX_BUDGET_USD_PER_ITERATION = $0.60 cut off all 3 reviewer iterations (~$0.61 each) before a verdict could be emitted; the loop exhausted its send-back cap with zero verdicts, requiring manual operator merge. The dev-loop's budgets were appropriate — only the reviewer's was mismatched.

nodes.label: trafficGame — stale brain themes that contradict the codebase cause PM-phase failure
nodes.description: Two 2026-05-10 themes still described the deleted CampaignLevels.ts array. The PM read the brain (8 reads), ingested a false model, Glob'd the real tree, hit an irreconcilable contradiction, and exhausted its entire budget producing hallucinated work items. Stale-but-contradictory brain content is worse than a gap.

nodes.label: Binary elevation model — one elevation per vehicle, three transition rules, every consumer reads the same value
nodes.description: After three failed attempts (3-level coloring, body-aware footprint that spans both levels, route-segment span elevations), the model that works is binary — vehicle.currentElevation is a single point, locked to ramp target on transition segments, lifted early at next-waypoint and ramp-CP boundaries. Collision, overlap, and IDM leader-finding all read the same value. Future-segment elevations evaluated at TARGET, not source-to-target span.

nodes.label: Grading-frontier infrastructure — parametric sweeps + locked baselines + curated screenshots as the tight-loop layer for map-design ideation
nodes.description: scripts/grading/runSweep.mjs is the reusable parallel sweep library; ~30 lines per new theory. docs/baselines/ locks the headline numbers. docs/baselines/screenshots/ is the curated visual reference. Together they make "is this design better" a measurable score-delta, not a prose argument.

nodes.label: trafficGame — algorithm-heavy items must be decomposed before queueing
nodes.description: Steiner topology + graph colouring as single work units caused 48% failure rate in v1 Cycle 3. Multi-file algorithmic restructuring exceeds the developer-loop's atomic-item design point.

nodes.label: trafficGame — canvas + BPR flow regressions need visual tests
nodes.description: Vitest unit tests miss canvas rendering and BPR-flow prediction regressions. Playwright test:visual is the orchestrator-verified gate for the game's correctness.

nodes.label: trafficGame — per-map star thresholds are hand-tuned, not auto-generated
nodes.description: Each map's star thresholds are calibrated against realistically achievable scores via playtesting. Auto-generating from heuristics produces frustrating-or-trivial maps.
